displayTitle: 2026-05-07 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Reserve Bank of New Zealand’s Financial Stability Report, May 2026
topic: Reserve Bank of New Zealand’s Financial Stability Report, May 2026
description: Thursday 7 May 2026| Finance and Expenditure Committee Reserve Bank of New Zealand’s Financial Stability Report, May 2026 Reserve Bank of New Zealand Dr Anna Breman, Governor Angus McGregor, Acting Assistant Governor Financial Stability Chris McDonald, Manager System Monitoring and Analysis Jess Rowe, Director Prudential Policy
descriptionSummary: Reserve Bank of New Zealand’s Financial Stability Report, May 2026. Reserve Bank of New Zealand. Dr Anna Breman, Governor

chunks.text: businesses and households, and that is what we expect in the near future. So thank you, and we look forward to taking your questions. Thank you, Governor. We'll kick off with Barbara Edmonds. Tena koutou katoa. Thank you, Governor, for your opening comments. Just wanting to have a look at three different lines of questioning, but I can ask the other two, Mr.

Chair, a little bit later just to allow time. My first line of questioning is around, obviously last year's Financial Stability Report in November, we were looking at impacts around what tariffs were doing on our domestic market, the adjustment by our firms in response to that. This year, obviously, recovering from a very low standard and some recovery there, and now we have the Iran conflict.

More domestically, what is it that we can do in New Zealand given that I'm noticing much more commentary now around the potential for stagflation, so low growth, high inflation. We also have unemployment numbers that came out yesterday, not exactly exciting. Yesterday may have dropped by 0.1%, but when you dig into the statistics, higher unemployment in Auckland and Wellington, our urban centers, as well as for our non-employment education and training, so our young people.

What would be if we move into, say for example, a stagflation setting, and I'm not saying we are in one now, but I'm hearing a lot of commentary about it. Without looking towards say a Volcker shock, which what happened in the US, what are some of the solutions that, for example, the Financial Policy Committee would be suggesting to ministers around how to... We want to ensure that we don't have higher unemployment because it's already high enough.

We've got a lot child poverty as well. Those standards have also increased. How do we actually deal with this? Because the diversity of our economy is, and low productivity has been an issue for a number of decades. What would be your solution or what would the Finance Policy Committee, what are the sort of solutions they'd be looking to advise ministers on?

Well, thank you for that question. So the first thing is that we did see before the conflict in the Middle East that the New Zealand economy was recovering, and we do expect now to see somewhat weaker growth. But we will comment more on the growth outlook and inflation when we come to the Monetary Policy Report later in May.

I think from a financial policy perspective, what we're highlighting in the report is that the data that we have in terms of firms being listed as high risk from the bank perspective and risk of default, that had come down significantly across many different sectors of the economy.

So I think the key thing right now is that the banks have the ability to keep on supporting both firms and households with their lending and be flexible in how they support the households. And what we see in the data on the banks' position is that they are in a good position to do this. They do have both the liquidity and the capital buffers.

So from a financial stability perspective, we actually do have some strength in New Zealand right now in the sense that we have ability to keep on supporting firms and households. And importantly, some of the things that we mention in the report is on the margins and the importance of keep on looking at different ways of ensuring that competition is strong enough so that the margins that we see...

We have a special topic on small businesses and medium-sized businesses, and there we see that the lending margins are higher than they are, for example, in Australia. So I think continue working with what we have done in the past few years with competition to reduce those margins and make sure that households and firms get access to capital will be very important in this environment.

I don't know if my colleagues would like to add. Governor, I might just add, it does highlight, as the Governor alluded to, the importance of the work that we do, thinking about our financial stability mandate and our prudential regulation responsibilities, the work that we do through the whole cycle to ensure that we're calibrating our settings in the most appropriate way to support resilience, such that when crises like these occur, the system is as well-placed as it possibly can be to weather these shocks through the cycle. So, the most important thing we can do is really

staying laser-focused on our financial stability mandate and making sure that we have those right settings in place. And sorry, if I can ask a supplementary, Mr. Chair, if that's okay. Sure. Because you mentioned the small business and obviously that the banks, they're still comfortable with it, but obviously their risk weighting will be different for say borrowing for residential property.

So I meet a number of small businesses, and particularly at a business breakfast I had last week with a number of advisors who advise small businesses, it seems to be that there's been no shift for small businesses in accessing capital. They still say, "We still have to go to the bank, but it's still only easier to go to the bank to access capital if we have a house that can be secured against it." What are the types of competition policy which should we be looking at as legislators to help support small business accessing capital?

Yeah. So I think one of the things that happened in the capital review that was decided on in December is that we will have more granular risk weightsAnd that will make it actually easier to have the appropriate risk weights, and that will help lending for different types of businesses. And the other thing is what we do stress in the report is transparency, because for mortgages, there is a lot of transparency, and households can compare across different banks, but that is more difficult for small and medium sized firm to get the same transparency.

So if we can get more transparency, it would be easier for SMEs to really get the opportunity to compare, and that will increase competition across the banks. So that's what we're advocating in this report for that reason. And what's the response been from the banks, for example?

Are they open to working with you? When do you see the sort of transparency starting to kind of be more public so that the potential borrower can actually start to compare the different products between the banks? Because this has been an issue for small businesses for a while. So trying to see some sort of movement in it is something that I know that they're calling for quite strongly.

Yeah. No, so we haven't seen the response yet from the FSRS of yesterday. But I don't know if you would like to address- Chris, why don't you... So the thing that comes to mind for me is just the project that we've been working on around the loan level data, and so we're collecting that from banks, and that will come available from around October later this year.

And so that really creates some opportunities for us to be able to dig much more deeply into the data, more granular, and improve our reporting. So helping to add to that transparency that we've been talking about. And in that project, I think the banks have been working with us really well. So, yeah, there's good opportunities to come with some of those things that are already in train.

Yeah, I'd echo that. Very good collaboration with the banks in relation to that loan level data initiative. Thank you. I'll come back later, Ryan, just allow for one other- Yeah. Thanks. Barb, I'd actually just like to ask a sup on this, and if anyone else has got a sup specific around small business, I'm happy to just jump. Otherwise, I'll go back to Chloe. But just on that transparency, do you have any recommendations around approaches like standardized disclosure, benchmarking, portability, and has any of the open banking shift started to flow through into

that space? Just be interested in your view on that. And also, is it within your remit or also Comcom, or other entities to help sort of solicit that? The open banking is advancing, is the information that we're getting, but I'm going to ask my colleagues to comment a bit more on the transparency with the loan level data that we'll have.

We can clearly contribute to that with the way that we report and send out the information. But on the open banking, Angus or Chris, would you like to comment more exactly on the status? Because I know it's advancing. Yeah. Jess might want to jump in here as well.

As you know, Chair, the open banking is very much being led by MB. We've been involved in a supporting role in that context. We're very supportive of the work that's going on there. It's really important and good to see banks getting on board with that. But again, the transparency point is the focus we've had is very much in relation to this loan level data exercise and really digging into the analysis that we've identified in the report this time, and we look forward to shining a light on that more over the coming months and years as we really improve our

access to data. Thank you. Chloe Swarbrick's got a supplementary on this. Yeah, just really wanted to dig into that point around SME borrowing, and particularly, I believe it's on page 20 of your report, you're speaking to how housing market dynamics can influence SME lending because so many SME owners are often borrowing against their house, which is obviously a common product- Yes... that's provided by many banks.

Governor, I'm sure you've heard the caricature of our economy as a housing market with bits tacked on the side. This is a challenge that we've faced for a really long time. However, your analysis kind of lends itself to the fact that what we're looking at here is not only real problems with the housing market and how much capital is flowing into that, but also the flow on effects with regard to SME lending and therefore our productive economy as well.

I was wondering if you could just unpack for us, I guess, some of the challenges that you potentially foresee if house prices are to further decrease. Will that then mean that there is a risk that banks' ability to lend to productive small businesses also further reduces? And how do we deal with that, if so? Yeah. I'm very aware of the fact that many SMEs do use their home as collateral in terms of loans that is then used for small businesses.

What we've seen now is that house prices have been flat overall for a number of years, and then there's quite some regional differences where parts of Southern Island, parts of the rural economy has been performing better. We've seen house prices going up, and it's been weaker around Auckland and Wellington in particular.

What we do highlight is that if we saw a worse outcome than we expect now, because now we still expect prices to remain about flat or actually start increase a little bit over time, then I don't think that we'll see a large impact as long as there's collateral in the house. But if we see a much worse outcome, that would again hit this. The other thing that is good, though, is that the more granular risk weights-Will actually help because it will get better data in terms of an ability to lend to households as well.

The other thing is that we did change it to the macro-prudential framework last year. So we changed the LTV, the loan to value ratio, and eased that somewhat, and that means that banks can actually increase their lending as compared to loan to value ratio.

They can make an exception for a larger share than they had before. So that's one of the things that we've done recently to make it easier for banks to lend to households. But I guess if I can just final sup on this, Mr. Chair, if I may? Yep. Yeah. So I guess just to put that as simply as possible to the, I don't know, seven-odd people who may potentially be tuned in, I guess, is there a risk that if house prices continue to flatline or to drop, that that has a negative impact on productivity in this country?

I think making a direct line from a part of the economy where house prices might not perform as well to overall productivity, it's many steps in there. So as an economist, I would be cautious, because I think there are many other things that could be on the upside in term of productivity and just in terms of investment in more digital services and a lot of other- Renewable energy... infrastructure that are coming in.

So I would be a little bit cautious to say that, but I do think that overall the housing market is important for SMEs. What we have done is made it easier, both through the risk weights and through the loan to value ratio, to increase lending. And I think going forward, the main scenario is still that we'll see prices moving sideways or somewhat higher.

And if we do have time, we may get to the structural reforms that were alluded to by Paul Conway in a recent speech. But if I may, to my primary now, Mr. Chair. No, we've got-- Yeah, I'll go to Deputy Governor Grosser for a sup. I just want to go to a sup on the SMEs as well, and it's to do with the ANZ card spending data that came out yesterday or the day before, and there was an alarmist headline that said card spending fell off a cliff, and spending was going backwards in apparel, interestingly in groceries, and in a variety of areas, but people were spending more on fuel.

So that's unsurprising. It just started to ring alarm bells for me with respect to our SMEs, to the extent to which they are going to be challenged by this fall in spending, and the extent to which banks will be in a position to hopefully support them through it. So I wonder if you have any comments around that whole scenario.

Yeah. So again, I'm trying to keep to the financial stability perspective and not move too much towards the next monetary policy meeting. But it's true that with higher fuel prices, now in the near term, we do expect that to carve out some other spending, and that's also what you see in that card transaction data that we see, and that is often small businesses being affected, retailers, restaurants, hotels, hospitality, et cetera.

I'm sorry. So I think that what we do see and what we do lift in this report is that what we've seen just before, up until the data we have, which includes March, there was much fewer small firms or medium-sized firms or businesses overall that were considered high risk by the banks.

So we're going into this with a period where the situation for banks in terms of the risks of defaults and people not being able to pay their loans were actually declining. So we are in a position where the banks, both in terms of having had a few years now where the defaults were declining and they also have very strong liquidity and capital buffer.

So it means that the banks do have the ability right now to support the small businesses and medium-sized business throughout the coming few months that are likely to be the toughest, until we hopefully see things starting to recover again. So the banks are really in a strong position to support SMEs right now.

Thank you. And if I could just add to that. Yes, please. One of the points we make in the report, and this is very much echoed by the banks themselves, is that where businesses or households are finding that they're stretched, that if there are risks in relation to concerns about repayment and debt, to engage with your bank early, swiftly, constructively.

The banks are very much on message on that point as well. So I think that's a really important message to get out. Thank you. We'll go to a new primary now from Chloe Swarbrick. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Now, I appreciate that this, of course, is not the monetary policy statement, but of course, there is a synthesis between that and what we are discussing today.

Just on the point that you made in introductory remarks around constantly just assessing the international arena and context and constantly calibrating settings, just wanted to get your take on something from the Australia Institute. So you'll be aware that two or three days ago now, the Reserve Bank of Australia made the decision to increase the interest rate rise. And I just want to read from the Australia Institute who said, "Higher interest rates will do nothing to open the Strait of Hormuz.

Higher interest rates cannot change the world price of oil and bring down fuel prices. All this does is heap more pain on already stretched households." Is that analysis that the Reserve Bank here agrees with? So I really will not comment on monetary policy today, because the focus is on the financial stability report.

What we've said so far in our last statement is that we do expect somewhat higher near-term inflation. Medium-term inflation that's our job to ensure that it becomes low and stable again. And on growth, we also expect somewhat weaker growth, but we still expect growth this year. That's important.

And we are looking at a number of factors to see if the near-term higher inflation becomes embedded in the economy. And whether or not we do that, we will comment at our next meeting in May. Okay. So hearing you'd prefer not to talk about those things, perhaps then this is the open door to talk more about those structural reforms that I alluded to, which obviously Paul Conway addressed in a speech last month, I believe. On page eight, I believe, of your report, you say that the fossil fuel crisis, and our exposure to the fossil

fuel crisis, which obviously Fitch has also highlighted as our substantial dependence on energy imports may result in weaker economic growth and tightening conditions. Can you just elaborate on what those structural reforms are that are being highlighted by RBNZ as an opportunity for fiscal policy or for government to potentially deal with here, noting, of course, you don't want to talk about monetary policy and the limitations or efficacy thereof today?

Yeah. So I don't have Paul's speech ahead of me, but I do think that one thing that we as economists often stress is that if we get structural reforms that increase productivity, that will increase growth, and it also tends to be good for inflation over the medium term in terms of higher productivity means that you don't need to increase the prices as much because you're more productive, so you can get both higher wages without getting higher inflation. And then in terms of actual structural reforms, I think it would be more appropriate for Paul to

answer to his speech right now. Thank you. Okay, we're going to go to Dan Bidois. Hey. Thank you, Mr. Chair, thank you, Governor, and to your team for a comprehensive stability report and media online live stream yesterday. I thought that went really well. Hey, I just want to test the resilience that you're referring to a lot in the report, in particular the stress test scenarios that the bank is modeling.

I'm just wondering, I guess first question off the bat is what scenarios have you modeled, in particular in relation to the conflict in the Middle East, how severe those scenarios are modeled, and what the likely impact of those scenarios will be on the stability of the banking system in terms of capital markets, and the ability of banks to continue to lend?

Yeah. Well, thank you. So there are a number of things here I can comment on. So the first thing is that we did a stress test related to geopolitical uncertainty last year, and that included assuming that unemployment increased to 10.5%, GDP falling by 6.5, and house prices falling 35%. So it was a rather extreme scenario, and we don't expect that at all in the current situation. That's important to say.

What we did in this report is that we updated that scenario with our new capital settings that we introduced in December last year, and they're being phased in gradually now. And what we show in the report is that with the new capital settings, with the same assumptions on unemployment growth and house price fall, the banks still have capital which is above the minimum level. So even in that extreme scenario, they do have the ability to keep on supporting the economy and keep on lending, but they will eat into some of the buffers, but they're still above the

minimum buffers. The other thing that we're doing is that this year we will also update this scenario, including in a few features that are more directly linked to the current scenario. So I'll hand over to Angus to comment on that. Yeah. So- Thank you... the previous stress test was very much around geopolitical instability and trade challenges. The one that we're doing this year does, as the governor says, bring in more of the circumstances that we're dealing with at the moment in terms of, for example, a materially higher oil price than the one that we're seeing at the

moment. Importantly, that stress test is one that we're going to be doing with our counterparts in Australia, the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority. We think that's a really helpful exercise, particularly noting that 85% of our banking sector is Australian-owned. So doing this in conjunction with Australia and thinking about the impact on the New Zealand subsidiaries and the Australian group and parent, and where the linkages might be between those, we think will be very useful and very informative. So that's

about to be rolled out in terms of that stress testing. We'll be reporting on that later in the year, and we're very happy to discuss it further with the committee in due course. Thank you. Can I just clarify and confirm that, okay, so current stress testing of the banking system hasn't modeled for a higher level of oil price?What level of oil price have you modeled for at the moment in current stress test?

The current... Yeah, do you have the exact number, Angus or Chris? There wasn't- I don't think... there wasn't an explicit assumption- That's all right... on oil prices in last year's one, so when we put out the scenario for this year's stress test, the one we're working with the counterparts in Australia, that's about a month's time, and that'll include a bit more detail around exactly the assumption used there.

So it'll be a higher oil price, but it'll also be a sustained higher oil price. So you'll see, I guess, the difference between the two scenarios really highlight what the role of some of those pressures from high oil prices will be. But I would just make the point that the stress test we did last year was enormously helpful in informing our understanding of the current crisis because it was very much focused on geopolitical instability. And as the governor referred to, with really quite extreme impacts on GDP, unemployment, house prices, that really

do help inform our understanding of how our banking system would cope with prolonged or challenging geopolitical instability of the type we're seeing now, albeit with slightly different inputs. Okay, thank you for that. The second question I have is around our vulnerabilities to the wholesale financial market overseas, given that we've got such a high level of our Australian-owned banks require a huge amount of capital from overseas. How exposed is New Zealand banks to the funding requirements, and the

shock to, or a sustained level of increase in our funding requirements for our Australian-owned banks? Yeah. So first I have to say that currently, despite all the uncertainty that this recent conflict has meant, wholesale markets and overall financial markets have been performing remarkably well. We've seen very little stress in the system.

In terms of international linkages, there are some international linkages. The New Zealand banks have a lot of domestic funding through deposits, et cetera. So the exposure to the wholesale financial market is there, but it is a smaller proportion actually of their overall funding.

But we do mention that in a stress scenario where you see global rates increasing significantly, that could put some stress on the system. But that's also why it's so important that we have high liquidity and capital buffers in New Zealand, and we do have that. So even if we saw that kind of pressure, we do expect the banks to be resilient.

I don't know if you would like Chris to add- Just a couple things. So it's around about a fifth of banks overall funding that is through wholesale markets and offshore wholesale markets. The big difference now compared to, say, the GFC period is the amount of that funding that is now in longer-term terms, so that they don't have that rollover so regularly.

And so that just means that the need to go to market as often is much less than it was back then. So even if you do get disruptions, they have the flexibility to wait for better periods in the market, so they don't have to get either disruptions or pay a whole lot more. So they're in a much better position to be able to manage that.

They have the flexibility for that. They also have very strong liquidity positions as well. So they're in a good, strong funding position. Now as Governor mentioned, part of that is the fact that they've got really good growth in deposits over recent years. So that's a really strong part of their funding at the moment. Thanks. Mr Chair, if I can just- I'm going to go to Todd next.

Sorry, Dan. All right. Thanks, mate. Got to keep moving. Thank you, Mr Chair. I'm actually surprised Dan Bidwell didn't ask this question, but one of the things in the report I was most interested in was the Financial Policy Committee because obviously, as you know, this committee did the banking inquiry, and this was one of the topics that came out of that.

So as I understand, this is the first report that that committee would've signed off, but I'm just interested in hearing, yeah, how's that committee going? Are you finding it useful? What input is it having into reports like this, et cetera? Because obviously we were very pleased to see the Reserve Bank take up that idea. Yeah. Thank you so much for that question and highlighting that.

So this is indeed the first report with the new Financial Policy Committee. And there is a special box in the report on the FPC and who is on the FPC. So it's a combination of board members, I'm a member of course, and then we have two external members, Heidi Richard and Prasanna Gai.

And this was also my first financial stability report, although I've written a number of them in my previous jobs. But I think that it was really a strength to have the Financial Policy Committee. The report came up to them, and they had the opportunity to comment and give input. And we had a good discussion and I know that that added a number of things from, again, a lot from the international perspective because you can see in this report that we do see that there are some heightened financial stability risks on the global arena, not just from the Iran

conflict, but it's also the way artificial intelligence is moving so quickly. It's also private credit and the increase in the non-bank financial institutions on the global scale. We're not very exposed to that in New Zealand, but we still mention it, and some of those discussion became more highlightedThanks to comments from the Financial Policy Committee. So we are really happy to have the FPC now, and their contribution has been strong to this report.

And I would just add, it's been quite strong. We've currently had two meetings of the committee, so it's still very early days- Mm-hmm... canvassing a range of issues, including certain other policy matters. I'm very encouraged by the nature of the engagement that the committee has on these issues, the quality of the members of the committee.

I think it will be a really important addition to strengthening our approach to determining financial policy. That's really good to hear. And has having a couple of independent members been really useful to have that kind of external perspective and challenge, in a constructive way, some of the thinking?

We think so. So on page 15 in the report, you do have a bit about the background and also more information on Heidi Richards and Prasanna Gai. So Heidi Richards has worked in both Australia and the United States over many years, working on supervision and financial stability both at, well, APRA and the Federal Reserve. So she has an excellent background within this field.

And Prasanna Gai is a professor at Auckland. He's also on the MPC. So I think it's a good addition to have external members on the committee. Mm. Thanks, Tom. Dr Meagan Woods. Thank you. I've got terrible feedback. How's the- Yes, still pretty bad. Shall I come back to you in a minute? Yeah. Do. Okay.

Barbara. Good morning again. I just want to have just a discussion around the insurance part and the stress testing that you're doing as part of the Financial Stability Report. Obviously, raised it very early in this parliamentary term, and this committee, around the importance of insurance for financial stability risk.

Obviously, it requires a joint effort between local authorities, central government, and the insurers themselves, particularly as more insurers look at risk-based pricing. Was there any discussion around the other tool which insurers have because we want a highly insured New Zealand, given that when you look at our losses, it's second to Bangladesh in the world. Was there any discussion around another potential tool around parametric pricing for insurers and whether the insurers are looking at that as a potential because you don't want them to leave an area

unless they've got a lot of potential claims risk in that one particular area. But are insurers stepping through the different types of tools that they can use to provide coverage? I'll give you a little bit of background, and then I'll hand over to Angus to comment a bit more. But there are two things in this report.

The first one is on insurance affordability, where we do point out the risk of natural hazards in New Zealand and how insurance premiums has been higher after those events, tend to fall back after a while, but that we do see risks of even though insurance coverage now is high, there is a risk that we would see more uninsurance or underinsurance going forward.

And of course, that could potentially be a financial stability risk. We are also doing a reverse stress test on the insurance industry this year, but that's more focused on the health and life insurers. Sorry, I have to drink a little bit of water, so I'll take that opportunity to hand over to Angus to give more details.

Yeah. And just in reference to that work in relation to affordability, we're working very closely with our co-regulators and financial regulatory colleagues on that review. It's a really important review in terms of really digging into some of the issues and challenges around availability and affordability, getting more data to help better understand some of those availability and affordability challenges, and then think about what some of those policy responses might be in due course. So that review is very much underway.

Watch this space. I'm sure the committee will have opportunities to engage on that further. Specifically in relation to your question around parametric pricing, not specifically, but perhaps it might be an area for us to dig into in future reports. Yeah. Just a quick sup off that. Is there a reason why you don't want to stress test fire in general? Is that because of the government's review into insurance and that you're doing the reverse stress testing on just health and PMO?

In short, the answer to that is our aim is generally to cycle through general insurance and then life and health insurance year on year. So we did a significant general insurance stress test last year, you might remember, in relation to that really serious seismic risk event.

This year we're doing life and health, and then we'll come back to general insurance next year. Thank you. Meagan, should we come back to you? Let's try that. Let's try that. Hmm. It's long delay. Yeah, I think it's having a few screens on.

Do you want to maybe put something into the chat, Meagan, and we can maybe do it that way. Chloe. Yeah. So I just wanted to move to box B on page 31 of the Financial Stability Report, where you speak to how increasing climate change risks, rebuild costs, and better modeling of seismic hazards resulting in rising premiums and increasingly affecting the affordability of insurance, et cetera.

You also speak to how concerns over affordability, climate change, and dependence on global reinsurance means the situation could change quickly and warrants monitoring. I was wondering if you could explicitly spell out for us what those key things are that you are monitoring, and what it is that all responsible politicians should also be monitoring in that instance, especially noting how quickly things can change.

Yeah. So, this box on page 31 is looking into the work that we're doing together with the Council of Financial Regulators. So that does include the FMA, Comcom, and MB. And we're really pleased that this work is ongoing. It will highlight the issues that you mentioned in terms of the risk of under-insurance and un-insurance going forward.

We have seen, and we have the graphs here on how insurance premiums increased after earthquakes and also extreme weather events. And what we're looking for is collecting more data, and that is an ongoing process, to better highlight what the stressors are.

Last year in the FSR, we had a special box on the reinsurance side, showing that it has had an impact on premiums in New Zealand, but also that the market has improved lately. And then the whole job will give us an opportunity both to get better information and come up with suggestions in terms of policy. But with that said, I don't know if either of you would like to comment a bit more on the details.

Yeah. I'll just add, I think the work that's underway at the moment with this review is really exploring some of those areas that do need to be monitored in more depth. So what I would say is, we're really looking forward to coming back and providing more detail on that, not just us, the Reserve Bank, but more generally the- That's it... COFA group, and can provide a lot more detail as we're working that through at the moment in terms of the type of issues, the type of data that we're really trying to surface at the moment to build our collective

understanding at a system level about what some of these issues and challenges are. And I should note that this is very much being led by COFA, but working in collaboration with industry. So again, I would just say, look, watch this space. The work is very much underway, and we look forward to having further conversations with committee in due course. If I may, just finally on that point, Mr. Chair.

Yeah, so I completely appreciate that obviously there's limitations as to what RBNZ can say, and obviously also what COFA's up to and as far as this work goes, so just let me know if this is outside of the parameters. But obviously, one of the many things that is also touched on is the kind of approach to fiscal consolidation, and as you'll know, this government's approach is very much on the spending side of things.

And I guess just trying to articulate and understand the trade-offs therein, where, for sake of specific example, when we're talking about climate adaptation, we have it from an OIA, the Greens do, from MFN Treasury, that flood protection spending, for example, has been cut by 41% in this term of Parliament. That then potentially obviously exposes us to less resilience and therefore greater impacts of climate change weather events. But then the kind of trade-off, arguably from the political side of things, is potentially more debt being taken on or the

requirement for more revenue per that fiscal consolidation point. So I guess, how does the RBNZ kind of consider financial stability and the trade-offs between the need for climate adaptation, which in turn means that we have more resilient financial stability, and/or those points around kind of fiscal consolidation, and where do you see the line of the points or the arguments that RBNZ can actually make around the government's approach to fiscal policy?

I think our job, in this report is really the financial stability and the system-wide perspective, and stressing how important it is to make sure that we have good data to be able to form the policies going forward. So I think our role is really to work with other government agencies.

The Treasury is also in the Council of Financial Regulators, just to ensure that we have the right information so that we then can hand it over to parliamentarians to make the decisions that you have to make. Thanks, Governor. I'm just going to read out Dr.

Megan Woods's, she's put in the chat just because of those sound issues. Mm-hmm. "You note ongoing uncertainty about how long the Strait of Hormuz will remain effectively closed, and that even after reopening it, it will take time for supply conditions to normalize. How has the Bank incorporated prolonged high fuel prices and potential physical fuel shortage into its baseline and alternative scenarios for financial stability?

For example, I was talking to a protein food producer yesterday who was expecting increased costs, fertilizer and feed in 2027 and flowing from there, and will this be a long tail? Will this long tail be a part of the new scenarios in a few months' time?" Well, this is the very difficult question that we're all struggling with right now, to understand how long the conflict will last and what the implications will be.

And even when we see an end to the situation, how long will it take for things to normalize again afterwards? So from a financial stability perspective, the key things that matter will be if this means that we see slower growth and it means that some businesses become under pressure, and if they can keep on payingOn their debt. Or if we, in the worst-case scenarios, you'll see more liquidations even for businesses.

Currently, we're not seeing that. The data we have so far is actually showing that the defaults have been coming down. So we are watching this very closely. We do know that the banks- Sorry, Nancy, your microphone's on. Yeah. No worries. So the one thing is that we do not see this happening yet. We do see that the banks are in a strong position to support all types of businesses throughout this.

We also see in terms of if you look, for example, at the agricultural sector, that commodity prices are still very high. So we do expect that sector to, even though they are hurt by, of course, higher diesel prices, higher transport costs, they have been performing well over the past few years, and we expect commodity prices to stay at a high level that will help that sector going forward. But importantly, from financial stability perspective, the banks do have the resilience to support all types of businesses throughout this period.

Thank you. Is that thumbs up for you, Megan, or? Mm. That's okay. Thank you. Barbara Edmonds. Thank you. Maybe perhaps a question for Jess. Congratulations. I know it's been a long time coming in relation to the Deposit Takers Scheme. Can you just take us through a summary?

Because I know you've had drafting for the exposure drafts. You've got to have consultation soon around the standards. Just take us through how that implementation's going, sort of feedback that you're getting from deposit takers, including non-bank deposit takers. Because as you recall, as that was going through the select committee last time, as we were putting the legislation through, there was a lot of concern around proportionality.

So if you could just step us through that. But we are up to the standards obviously being released in June for further consultation. Yeah. I'm really happy to hand that over to Jess. Jess Rowe, please. Thank you very much. And yes, it has been sort of it's a multi-year project.

We're really pleased with how it's rolling out. Obviously, the legislation was passed in 2023. The Depositor Compensation Scheme was stood up mid last year, so that's implemented now. And as you mentioned, we are in the process of consulting on the suite of new prudential standards that for the first time will bring together the banks and the non-bank deposit taker sector. And it is underpinned by the proportionality framework that you'll recall we briefed the committee on and was actually inserted into the legislation by this

committee. The feedback we've had is really good from particularly the non-bank deposit taker sector. We've upped our engagement with them because we haven't been the direct supervisor of the sector before. They've joined our Council of Financial Regulators Deposit Takers forum, and we hold regular workshops with them. And we find the input that we get from them and the wider deposit-taking sector essential for refining our policies.

So it's great that we've made it to the stage where the legislation or the secondary legislation has been drafted and released for consultation. So we're all on track, looking forward to re-licensing and fully going live in 2028. Thank you. Probably just a couple of final questions from me, if I may, Governor, and probably a follow-on from Megan's question.

Obviously, it's a crystal ball in terms of duration with the crisis. But in terms of your central scenario, what are your nonlinear tipping points, e.g. oil price level, shipping disruption, et cetera, that will drive the downside tail?

So for the Financial Stability Report, we don't do that kind of general forecasting, so we will come back to that with our main monetary policy statement. But of course, in terms of the risks and seeing more severe effects on the New Zealand economy would be if we see prices staying high for a long period of time, or in the worst scenarios, some sort of direct supply disruptions in terms of fuel. But we do not see that as...

With the information we've had recently, we consider that it's likely for us to have access to fuels, but we could see a high price. But again, I really do not want to get into forecasting ahead of our main monetary policy meeting. But we are closely, of course, monitoring all these effects and talking to our international peers as well to get information on what they are seeing in this space.

Sure. And finally, what do you think is the single biggest risk that markets might be underpricing right now? So I think that some of the things that we highlight in terms of financial stability, where everyone is focusing and monitoring the situation in the Middle East very closely. I think that the other risk that we also highlight in terms of artificial intelligence and the risk of cyberattacks on the financial sector, or the increase in the non, what we call the non-bank financial intermediaries. Those are things like hedge funds, private

credit, et cetera. If we see any disturbances there, we know that New Zealand is not directly exposed. But internationally, this has grown a lot and that could cause turbulence in financial markets that would then spill into uncertainty for us as well. So I do think it's important to also monitor and follow those risks right now.

Yes. Thank you, Governor, and thank you Angus, Chris, and Jess for joining us today. We look forward to seeing you in a few weeks' time. Thank you so much. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Members, just a few items that we're just tabling that we can bring up when we meet each other next week. So on that note, have a fantastic rest of your day.
Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thanks, Mr. Chair. Thank you, Chair. Thanks, everyone.
